sections.heading: Clinical Note
sections.section_value:
Chief Complaint
Surgical consult for a biopsy of a suspicious skin lesion.
History of Present Illness
The patient's dermatologist referred her for a surgical consult following a biopsy of a 0.7 millimeter lesion on her right inferior back. She first noticed the spot approximately one month prior when visiting her mother. Her mother and sister have had melanoma. The patient reports no prior history of skin cancer. She denies any other cancers. She reports occasional smoking. She has had a left hand pain, but denies any other symptoms. She is currently not taking any medications and has no known medical problems. Her primary care physician has examined her moles annually and has not noted any concerns prior to this biopsy.
Findings
Examination reveals a one centimeter shave biopsy site on the right inferior back, including all of the dermis with no residual pigmentation. There are no intrinsic or satellite lesions. No other suspicious moles are identified. The right inguinal lymph node is palpable and has slightly atypical consistency. The remainder of the physical exam is unremarkable.
Assessment
The pathology report confirms an invasive, superficial spreading melanoma with a Breslow thickness of 0.7 millimeters. The lesion is staged as Stage 1.
Plan

sections.heading: Clinical Note
sections.section_value: I recommend a wide local excision with a one to two centimeter margin. Skin graft closure may be needed. We will obtain an ultrasound of the right inguinal lymph node to rule out metastatic disease. The patient will follow up with dermatology for continued monitoring. I will schedule the ultrasound and the procedure.
